speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Let's start with your vitals — your heart rate is 72 beats per minute, which is within the normal range.

speaker: patient
text: Good morning, thank you. I’ve been feeling a bit more fatigued than usual lately.

speaker: doctor
text: Any chest discomfort, shortness of breath, or palpitations?

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your history and today’s assessment, your primary diagnosis remains Coronary Artery Disease.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic blood pressure is 92 mmHg — slightly elevated, so we’ll keep an eye on that.

speaker: patient
text: Yes — actually, I’ve noticed swelling in my ankles and lower legs over the past few days.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s important. Lower limb edema can signal fluid retention — possibly related to cardiac function or medication effects. Any weight gain or increased shortness of breath when lying flat?

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin level is 118 g/L — mildly low, but not severely anemic. We’ll monitor this closely, especially given your CAD and current symptoms.

speaker: doctor
text: Your systolic blood pressure is 110 mmHg — that’s on the lower side, especially combined with your heart rate and edema.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also been lightheaded when standing up quickly, especially in the mornings.

speaker: doctor
text: That could be orthostatic hypotension — let’s check your BP both sitting and standing next time. Are you still taking metoprolol 50 mg twice daily?

speaker: patient
text: Yes, every morning and evening, as prescribed.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your fatigue, edema, and borderline-low BP, we need to consider whether your beta-blocker dose is contributing. Have you missed any doses recently?

speaker: patient
text: No, I haven’t missed any — but I did feel unusually tired last night and checked my pulse before bed. It was really slow.

speaker: doctor
text: How slow?

speaker: patient
text: I counted 54 — I double-checked just now and got 52.

speaker: doctor
text: Ah — your heart rate is now 52 bpm. That’s significantly lower than the 72 we recorded at the start of the visit. This bradycardia may explain your lightheadedness and fatigue.

speaker: doctor
text: I am going to switch you from metoprolol to ivabradine 5 mg twice daily — it lowers heart rate without affecting blood pressure as much, and should help reduce symptoms while maintaining cardiac protection.

speaker: patient
text: Okay, I’ll start that tomorrow. Should I still monitor my blood pressure?

speaker: patient
text: Yes — I just checked it again: systolic is still 110 mmHg, same as the reading you took earlier.
